title: Neck of Femur Fracture
oneLiner: A common fragility fracture of the proximal femur, typically in elderly osteoporotic patients after a low-energy fall, requiring urgent surgical decision-making to prevent avascular necrosis and enable early mobility.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
You are called to the emergency department for an 82-year-old woman who "slipped on the carpet" at home. She landed on her right hip and cannot stand. On examination, the right leg is shortened and externally rotated; any attempt at passive range of motion in the hip causes severe groin pain. She cannot actively straight-leg-raise. Her pre-morbid mobility was independent with a stick. Observations are stable but she looks distressed.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **82-year-old female** → postmenopausal osteoporosis; reduced bone density makes fracture from minimal trauma likely.
- **Low-energy mechanism (slip on carpet)** → classic for fragility fracture; high-energy (e.g., RTA) would raise suspicion for associated injuries or a different fracture pattern.
- **Shortened & externally rotated leg** → pathognomonic for a displaced intracapsular or intertrochanteric fracture: the distal fragment is pulled into external rotation by the short external rotators (piriformis, obturators) and shortened by the glutei and iliopsoas.
- **Inability to weight-bear** → confirms an unstable fracture; a non-displaced impacted fracture may allow limited weight-bearing, but always check.
- **Severe groin pain on any movement** → distinguishes hip fracture from hip dislocation or trochanteric bursitis; pain is referred along the obturator nerve to the groin.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **The impacted (Garden I) fracture** — patient may be able to walk (though with pain), leg not shortened or rotated. X-ray can be subtle; need MRI if suspicion high.
- **Stress fracture in a young runner** — presents with insidious groin pain, no fall, normal X-ray early; requires MRI for diagnosis. Miss it and it can displace into a full fracture.
- **Pathological fracture from metastasis** — minimal or no trauma; pain may be present for weeks; look for a lytic lesion on X-ray; multiple myeloma or breast cancer common culprits.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
A neck of femur (NOF) fracture is a break in the femoral neck, the narrow segment of bone just below the femoral head. It is **intracapsular** — the fracture line lies within the capsule of the hip joint. This distinction matters enormously because the blood supply to the femoral head enters through the capsule via the retinacular vessels (branches of the medial circumflex femoral artery). An intracapsular fracture can disrupt this supply, leading to avascular necrosis (AVN) of the femoral head, bone collapse, and poor outcomes.

Why it matters clinically:
- **Frequency:** Over 70,000 NOF fractures per year in the UK; one of the most common reasons for emergency orthopaedic admission in the elderly.
- **Stakes:** One-year mortality is ~25-30%; many patients never regain their pre-fracture mobility. Delayed surgery (>48h) increases complications (pressure sores, delirium, pneumonia).
- **Common pitfalls:** Missing an undisplaced fracture; failing to differentiate intracapsular from intertrochanteric (extracapsular) — management differs; under-treating osteoporosis post-fixation.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The blood supply story:**
The femoral head receives blood from three sources: the **medial circumflex femoral artery** (MCFA) provides the dominant supply via the retinacular vessels that run along the neck; the **lateral circumflex femoral artery** supplies the base; and the **artery of the ligamentum teres** (from the obturator artery) supplies the fovea but is often insufficient in adults. In a displaced intracapsular fracture, the retinacular vessels are torn, leaving the femoral head dependent on the tenuous ligamentum teres supply — unreliable. This explains why **displacement (Garden III-IV) carries a high risk of AVN**.

**Why shortening and external rotation occur:**
- **Muscle pulls:** The gluteus maximus and medius (inserting on the greater trochanter) pull the distal fragment proximally → shortening. The short external rotators (piriformis, obturator internus, gemelli) pull the distal fragment into external rotation.
- **Capsular tamponade:** In an intracapsular fracture, bleeding from the fracture site cannot escape (tight capsule), raising intra-articular pressure, further compromising femoral head blood flow. This is why an undisplaced fracture can still cause AVN if the capsule is tense.

**Why groin pain?** The hip joint is innervated by the obturator nerve (L2-L4) and branches from the femoral nerve. Pain from the femoral head/neck is referred to the groin and medial thigh via the obturator nerve. Pain on axial loading (e.g., heel strike) is classic — because the fracture is stressed.

**Why is inability to weight-bear so reliable?** In a displaced fracture, the two fragments are no longer in continuity; any axial load is transmitted across the fracture site, causing movement and pain. In an undisplaced impacted fracture, the fragments are still together and the patient may actually walk, but with a limp and pain.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Osteoporotic (most common)** | Low-energy fall in elderly woman with osteoporosis | "Tripped over a rug", pre-existing osteopenia, age >70 |
| **High-energy trauma** | RTA, fall from height in young adults | Young male, MVA, multi-trauma |
| **Stress fracture** | Repetitive loading in athletes, military recruits | "Insidious groin pain while running", normal X-ray early |
| **Pathological** | Metastasis (breast, lung, prostate, myeloma), Paget’s disease, local bone lesion | Minimal/no trauma, prior cancer, lytic lesion on X-ray |
| **Insulin deficiency / renal osteodystrophy** | Diabetes, chronic kidney disease | Higher risk of falls and poor bone quality |

**Highlight:** Osteoporosis from postmenopausal estrogen loss is the single most common underlying cause. Always screen for secondary causes (vitamin D deficiency, hyperparathyroidism, glucocorticoid use).
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:**
- **Garden classification** (for intracapsular NOF fractures) based on X-ray:
  - I: incomplete/impacted, valgus angulation
  - II: complete, non-displaced
  - III: complete, partially displaced (angulated, but fragments still contact)
  - IV: complete, fully displaced (no contact)
- **AO/OTA classification** also used but more complex; Garden is standard for clinical risk of AVN.

**Key investigations:**
| Test | What it shows | Why order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-------------|------------------|
| **AP Pelvis + lateral hip X-ray** | Fracture line, displacement, orientation (valgus/varus), neck-shaft angle | First-line, confirms the fracture in >95% | Garden I: impacted; Garden III-IV: displaced. Check for associated injuries (pubic rami, acetabulum). |
| **MRI hip** | Bone marrow oedema, occult fracture line (even in Garden I) | Suspected occult fracture with negative X-ray but high clinical suspicion | High signal on STIR/T2 fat-suppressed sequences confirms fracture. Also detects AVN early (low signal on T1). |
| **CT hip** | Cortical disruption, comminution, pathological lesion | If MRI contraindicated or to plan complex trauma/pathological fracture | Shows fracture anatomy; can miss marrow oedema. |
| **FBC, U&E, LFT, coagulation** | Baseline haemoglobin, renal function, electrolytes | Pre-operative assessment; check for anaemia (chronic disease) or coagulopathy (warfarin) | Hb <100 g/L may need transfusion. Cr >150 may alter implant choice (risk of ARF from nephrotoxic drugs). |
| **Group & screen, cross-match** | Blood group, antibody screen | Prepare for blood loss (~500 mL intra-op for hemiarthroplasty) | Positive antibody screen complicates cross-matching; order early. |
| **ECG** | Arrhythmias, ischaemia | Pre-op cardiac risk assessment; common in elderly | Atrial fibrillation may need rate control; acute ischaemia → cardiology consult. |
| **Chest X-ray** | Pneumonia, effusion, heart failure | Often done as part of pre-op workup | New opacity may delay surgery. |
| **DEXA scan** | Bone mineral density (BMD) | Post-fixation to diagnose osteoporosis and guide treatment | T-score < -2.5 = osteoporosis; need bisphosphonate therapy. |

**Pattern recognition — what single finding confirms or rules out:**
- **X-ray clearly showing a fracture line through the femoral neck** = confirm. But **a negative X-ray does NOT rule out** — if clinical suspicion remains, go to **MRI**.
- **Bruising or swelling over the lateral hip** is more typical of intertrochanteric fractures (blood can track outside the capsule). In intracapsular NOF fractures, swelling is minimal because the capsule contains the haematoma.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**
- **Analgesia:** Paracetamol 1g IV/PO + morphine sulphate 2.5-5mg IV (titrate to pain) or use a **femoral nerve block** (20mL 0.25% levobupivacaine) → excellent pain relief without respiratory depression. *Why?* Pain causes immobility, delirium, hypertension; nerve block reduces need for systemic opioids.
- **IV fluids:** 500-1000mL crystalloid unless heart failure. *Why?* Patient may be nil-by-mouth for surgery; preload prevents hypotension.
- **Remove traction** — traditional skin traction is **not recommended** (increases pain and pressure sores). *Pitfall:* Don’t apply traction; it does not improve outcome.
- **Assess for acute medical issues:** ECG, chest X-ray, bloods. *Why?* Identify decompensated HF, AF, pneumonia, anaemia that need optimization before theatre.
- **Notify anaesthetist and orthopaedic team** for expedited surgery. Target surgery within 48 hours (NICE guidance) — reduces mortality and complications.

**Ward (24-72h):**
- **Surgical decision:** Based on fracture type, patient mobility, and cognition.
  - **Undisplaced intracapsular (Garden I-II):** **Cannulated screws (Hansson/Pins)** — percutaneous fixation with 2-3 screws. Preserves the joint.
  - **Displaced intracapsular (Garden III-IV):**
    - **Patient >65 years, low mobility, pre-existing arthritis → Hemiarthroplasty** (replaces femoral head only). Choose cemented stem (less thigh pain, better fixation) or uncemented (if medical comorbidities prevent cement use — risk of cement reaction syndrome).
    - **Patient <65 years, high mobility, good bone quality → Total hip replacement (THR)** — better long-term outcome and lower risk of revision.
  - **Intertrochanteric (extracapsular):** **Sliding hip screw (SHS)** for stable patterns; **intramedullary nail (IM nail)** for unstable patterns (reverse obliquity, subtrochanteric extension).
- **Thromboprophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily for 28-35 days (if CrCl >30) or fondaparinux 2.5mg SC. *Why?* High risk of DVT/PE after hip fracture (incidence up to 60% without prophylaxis). Mechanical prophylaxis (stockings) can be used but LMWH is standard.
- **Mobilise day 1 post-op** with physiotherapy, weight-bearing as tolerated (for all fixation types except certain complex cases). *Why?* Prevents chest infection, muscle wasting, pressure sores. **Pitfall:** Delaying mobilisation increases complications.
- **Osteoporosis workup:** Start vitamin D 800IU daily + calcium 1g daily. Arrange DEXA scan 3 months post-op. Start bisphosphonate (alendronate 70mg PO weekly) once fracture healed (usually 2-4 weeks). *Why?* Second hip fracture risk is 20% within 5 years.
- **Delirium prevention:** Avoid benzodiazepines; ensure good pain control; regular orientation; early removal of catheters; keep glasses and hearing aids.

**Long-term:**
- **Falls risk assessment:** Identify home hazards, consider occupational therapy home visit.
- **Osteoporosis treatment continuation:** Bisphosphonate for 3-5 years; review yearly.
- **Follow-up X-ray** at 6 weeks and 3 months to assess union. If non-union or AVN develops, may need revision.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | NOF Fracture (Intracapsular) | Hip Dislocation (Posterior) | Trochanteric Bursitis | Osteoarthritis (Coxarthrosis) |
|---------|-----------------------------|-----------------------------|-----------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Acute, groin, on any hip motion; inability to weight-bear | Severe, at hip; leg held in internal rotation, adduction, shortened | Lateral hip pain, worse lying on side, tender over greater trochanter | Chronic, groin/buttock pain, worse with walking, stiffness |
| **Exam** | Shortened + externally rotated leg; painful passive ROM | Shortened + internally rotated leg; femoral head not palpable in posterior dislocation | Pain on resisted abduction; full ROM in non-inflammatory stage | Crepitus, reduced internal rotation first, fixed flexion deformity |
| **X-ray** | Fracture line through femoral neck; displacement seen | Femoral head out of acetabulum; usually posterior | Normal (may see trochanteric calcific tendinitis) | Joint space narrowing, osteophytes, subchondral cysts |
| **Labs** | Normal (unless pathological) | Normal | Normal | Normal |
| **Key differentiator** | Fracture line on X-ray; external rotation with shortening | Internal rotation + shortening; head not in joint on X-ray | Point tenderness over trochanter; pain with resisted abduction; no groin pain | Chronicity; X-ray changes; normal acute markers |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Avascular Necrosis (AVN) of the femoral head**
- **Presentation:** Delayed — months to years post-fracture. Worsening groin pain, limp, inability to weight-bear. May follow initial good recovery.
- **Key signs:** X-ray shows increased radiodensity (sclerosis) of the femoral head, later collapse/flattening (Ficat stages). MRI is definitive (low T1 signal, double-line sign on T2).
- **What to do:** Early AVN: core decompression or vascularised fibular graft in young patients. Late AVN: THR.

**Non-union**
- **Presentation:** Pain at fracture site >6 months post-op, inability to walk comfortably.
- **Key signs:** X-ray shows no bridging callus, gap at fracture site, implant loosening.
- **What to do:** Usually requires revision fixation, often with bone grafting or conversion to arthroplasty.

**Deep wound infection / periprosthetic joint infection**
- **Presentation:** Persistent pain, wound discharge, fever, raised CRP (can be >100).
- **Key signs:** Erythema, purulent drainage, sinus tract. Aspiration shows organism (Staph aureus most common).
- **What to do:** I&D with retention of implant (if early, <2 weeks) or 2-stage revision (remove implant, antibiotic spacer, then re-implant after 6 weeks of IV antibiotics).

**DVTs / PE**
- **Presentation:** Unilateral leg swelling, warmth, tenderness (DVT); sudden dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, hypoxia, collapse (PE).
- **Key signs:** Wells score >2; D-dimer positive but not specific; CTPA confirms PE.
- **What to do:** Therapeutic LMWH (enoxaparin 1.5mg/kg SC daily) or DOAC (rivaroxaban 15mg BD). Consider IVC filter if anticoagulation contraindicated.

**Pressure sores**
- **Presentation:** Discoloration, ulceration over sacrum, heels, elbows.
- **What to do:** Regular turning (4-hourly), pressure-relieving mattress, early mobilisation. *Pitfall:* Delay to theatre increases risk — skin can break within 24h.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**
- **GARDEN** for intracapsular fracture classification:
  - **G**rade 1 – impacted
  - **A**ngulated (Grade 3 – partially displaced)
  - **R**eally displaced (Grade 4 – fully displaced)
  - **D**on’t forget Grade 2 (complete but undisplaced)
  - **E**valuation of blood supply risk (Grades 3&4 high)
  - **N**ecessary management decision (replacement vs fixation)
- **LEGS** for signs of displaced hip fracture:
  - **L**eg shortened
  - **E**xternal rotation
  - **G**roin pain
  - **S**upport (cannot weight-bear)
- **3Ms** for complications to watch: Mortality (30%), Morbidity (delirium, PE), Mobility loss.

**Clinical Pearls:**
1. An elderly patient who cannot straight-leg-raise after a fall has a hip fracture until proven otherwise — even with normal X-ray. Order an MRI.
2. The most reliable sign of an impacted (Garden I) fracture is **pain on axial loading through the foot** (heel strike) with no leg deformity.
3. Always check the **contralateral hip** — patients with one hip fracture have a 20% risk of a second within 5 years.
4. Never give an NSAID for pain in a patient with renal impairment (common in elderly) — risk of AKI.
5. In a patient on warfarin, reverse with vitamin K (2.5-5mg IV) and/or prothrombin complex concentrate if urgent surgery needed — but weigh risk of thrombosis.
6. The most commonly missed fracture on X-ray is the **non-displaced intracapsular** — the only clue may be a subtle disruption of Shenton’s line (the curve formed by the obturator foramen and femoral neck).
7. **Delirium** is the most common complication (>50% of patients) — often multi-factorial (pain, sleep deprivation, dehydration, infection). Don't blame “dementia” — address causes.

**Common Ward / Exam Mistakes:**
- Ordering a **CT scan instead of MRI** for an occult hip fracture — CT has lower sensitivity for marrow oedema; you may miss it.
- Applying **skin traction** — it increases pain and pressure sores without benefit.
- Assuming a **normal X-ray after trauma** means no fracture — if patient cannot weight-bear, get MRI.
- Delaying **osteoporosis treatment** — start vitamin D and calcium in hospital; arrange DEXA before discharge.
- Not checking **pre-operative group & save** until the night before — delays surgery.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call your senior (Registrar or Consultant):**
- **NEWS2 score ≥5** — especially if high respiratory rate, low SpO2, or hypotension.
- **Acute confusion / agitation** not settling with simple measures — could be hypoxia, PE, hypoglycaemia, or infection.
- **Chest pain or sudden dyspnoea** — suspect PE (especially day 3-7 post-op).
- **Hypoxia (SpO2 <92% on air)** — check for PE, pneumonia, atelectasis.
- **Temperature ≥38.5°C** — likely infection (chest, urine, wound); note that fracture alone can cause mild fever (day 1-2) but should not persist.
- **Uncontrolled pain** despite nerve block and systemic analgesia — may indicate compartment syndrome (rare in hip), infection, or patient anxiety.
- **Wound oozing or haematoma** with signs of bleeding (tachycardia, hypotension) — may need return to theatre.

**When to call the ITU/MET/CCOT:**
- **NEWS2 ≥7** in any patient.
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** unexplained.
- **GCS ≤12** or acute drop of ≥2 points.
- **Respiratory rate >30 or <8**.
- **Systolic BP <90 mmHg despite fluid bolus** — suspect septic shock, PE, or bleeding.
- **Cardiac arrest** — immediately call 2222.

**Specific thresholds to remember:**
- **Hb <70 g/L** (or <80 in active ischaemia) → consider transfusion.
- **Creatinine increase >26 µmol/L in 24h** or >50% from baseline → suspect AKI; discuss with renal team.
- **INR >1.5** pre-op → reverse with vitamin K (and consider PCC if need urgent surgery).
- **Potassium <2.5 or >6.5 mmol/L** → urgent correction and ECG; discuss with med reg.

Remember: The senior clinician can often be reached by simply stating the NEWS2, the problem, and your concern — “Mr/Smith has NEWS 6, with acute hypoxia and confusion day 3 post-hip fracture; I’m worried about a PE.”

## Neck of Femur Fracture
**One-liner:** A common fragility fracture of the proximal femur, typically in elderly osteoporotic patients after a low-energy fall, requiring urgent surgical decision-making to prevent avascular necrosis and enable early mobility.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
You are called to the emergency department for an 82-year-old woman who "slipped on the carpet" at home. She landed on her right hip and cannot stand. On examination, the right leg is shortened and externally rotated; any attempt at passive range of motion in the hip causes severe groin pain. She cannot actively straight-leg-raise. Her pre-morbid mobility was independent with a stick. Observations are stable but she looks distressed.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **82-year-old female** → postmenopausal osteoporosis; reduced bone density makes fracture from minimal trauma likely.
- **Low-energy mechanism (slip on carpet)** → classic for fragility fracture; high-energy (e.g., RTA) would raise suspicion for associated injuries or a different fracture pattern.
- **Shortened & externally rotated leg** → pathognomonic for a displaced intracapsular or intertrochanteric fracture: the distal fragment is pulled into external rotation by the short external rotators (piriformis, obturators) and shortened by the glutei and iliopsoas.
- **Inability to weight-bear** → confirms an unstable fracture; a non-displaced impacted fracture may allow limited weight-bearing, but always check.
- **Severe groin pain on any movement** → distinguishes hip fracture from hip dislocation or trochanteric bursitis; pain is referred along the obturator nerve to the groin.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **The impacted (Garden I) fracture** — patient may be able to walk (though with pain), leg not shortened or rotated. X-ray can be subtle; need MRI if suspicion high.
- **Stress fracture in a young runner** — presents with insidious groin pain, no fall, normal X-ray early; requires MRI for diagnosis. Miss it and it can displace into a full fracture.
- **Pathological fracture from metastasis** — minimal or no trauma; pain may be present for weeks; look for a lytic lesion on X-ray; multiple myeloma or breast cancer common culprits.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
A neck of femur (NOF) fracture is a break in the femoral neck, the narrow segment of bone just below the femoral head. It is **intracapsular** — the fracture line lies within the capsule of the hip joint. This distinction matters enormously because the blood supply to the femoral head enters through the capsule via the retinacular vessels (branches of the medial circumflex femoral artery). An intracapsular fracture can disrupt this supply, leading to avascular necrosis (AVN) of the femoral head, bone collapse, and poor outcomes.

Why it matters clinically:
- **Frequency:** Over 70,000 NOF fractures per year in the UK; one of the most common reasons for emergency orthopaedic admission in the elderly.
- **Stakes:** One-year mortality is ~25-30%; many patients never regain their pre-fracture mobility. Delayed surgery (>48h) increases complications (pressure sores, delirium, pneumonia).
- **Common pitfalls:** Missing an undisplaced fracture; failing to differentiate intracapsular from intertrochanteric (extracapsular) — management differs; under-treating osteoporosis post-fixation.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings
**The blood supply story:**
The femoral head receives blood from three sources: the **medial circumflex femoral artery** (MCFA) provides the dominant supply via the retinacular vessels that run along the neck; the **lateral circumflex femoral artery** supplies the base; and the **artery of the ligamentum teres** (from the obturator artery) supplies the fovea but is often insufficient in adults. In a displaced intracapsular fracture, the retinacular vessels are torn, leaving the femoral head dependent on the tenuous ligamentum teres supply — unreliable. This explains why **displacement (Garden III-IV) carries a high risk of AVN**.

**Why shortening and external rotation occur:**
- **Muscle pulls:** The gluteus maximus and medius (inserting on the greater trochanter) pull the distal fragment proximally → shortening. The short external rotators (piriformis, obturator internus, gemelli) pull the distal fragment into external rotation.
- **Capsular tamponade:** In an intracapsular fracture, bleeding from the fracture site cannot escape (tight capsule), raising intra-articular pressure, further compromising femoral head blood flow. This is why an undisplaced fracture can still cause AVN if the capsule is tense.

**Why groin pain?** The hip joint is innervated by the obturator nerve (L2-L4) and branches from the femoral nerve. Pain from the femoral head/neck is referred to the groin and medial thigh via the obturator nerve. Pain on axial loading (e.g., heel strike) is classic — because the fracture is stressed.

**Why is inability to weight-bear so reliable?** In a displaced fracture, the two fragments are no longer in continuity; any axial load is transmitted across the fracture site, causing movement and pain. In an undisplaced impacted fracture, the fragments are still together and the patient may actually walk, but with a limp and pain.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Osteoporotic (most common)** | Low-energy fall in elderly woman with osteoporosis | "Tripped over a rug", pre-existing osteopenia, age >70 |
| **High-energy trauma** | RTA, fall from height in young adults | Young male, MVA, multi-trauma |
| **Stress fracture** | Repetitive loading in athletes, military recruits | "Insidious groin pain while running", normal X-ray early |
| **Pathological** | Metastasis (breast, lung, prostate, myeloma), Paget’s disease, local bone lesion | Minimal/no trauma, prior cancer, lytic lesion on X-ray |
| **Insulin deficiency / renal osteodystrophy** | Diabetes, chronic kidney disease | Higher risk of falls and poor bone quality |

**Highlight:** Osteoporosis from postmenopausal estrogen loss is the single most common underlying cause. Always screen for secondary causes (vitamin D deficiency, hyperparathyroidism, glucocorticoid use).

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
**Diagnostic criteria:**
- **Garden classification** (for intracapsular NOF fractures) based on X-ray:
  - I: incomplete/impacted, valgus angulation
  - II: complete, non-displaced
  - III: complete, partially displaced (angulated, but fragments still contact)
  - IV: complete, fully displaced (no contact)
- **AO/OTA classification** also used but more complex; Garden is standard for clinical risk of AVN.

**Key investigations:**
| Test | What it shows | Why order it | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-------------|------------------|
| **AP Pelvis + lateral hip X-ray** | Fracture line, displacement, orientation (valgus/varus), neck-shaft angle | First-line, confirms the fracture in >95% | Garden I: impacted; Garden III-IV: displaced. Check for associated injuries (pubic rami, acetabulum). |
| **MRI hip** | Bone marrow oedema, occult fracture line (even in Garden I) | Suspected occult fracture with negative X-ray but high clinical suspicion | High signal on STIR/T2 fat-suppressed sequences confirms fracture. Also detects AVN early (low signal on T1). |
| **CT hip** | Cortical disruption, comminution, pathological lesion | If MRI contraindicated or to plan complex trauma/pathological fracture | Shows fracture anatomy; can miss marrow oedema. |
| **FBC, U&E, LFT, coagulation** | Baseline haemoglobin, renal function, electrolytes | Pre-operative assessment; check for anaemia (chronic disease) or coagulopathy (warfarin) | Hb <100 g/L may need transfusion. Cr >150 may alter implant choice (risk of ARF from nephrotoxic drugs). |
| **Group & screen, cross-match** | Blood group, antibody screen | Prepare for blood loss (~500 mL intra-op for hemiarthroplasty) | Positive antibody screen complicates cross-matching; order early. |
| **ECG** | Arrhythmias, ischaemia | Pre-op cardiac risk assessment; common in elderly | Atrial fibrillation may need rate control; acute ischaemia → cardiology consult. |
| **Chest X-ray** | Pneumonia, effusion, heart failure | Often done as part of pre-op workup | New opacity may delay surgery. |
| **DEXA scan** | Bone mineral density (BMD) | Post-fixation to diagnose osteoporosis and guide treatment | T-score < -2.5 = osteoporosis; need bisphosphonate therapy. |

**Pattern recognition — what single finding confirms or rules out:**
- **X-ray clearly showing a fracture line through the femoral neck** = confirm. But **a negative X-ray does NOT rule out** — if clinical suspicion remains, go to **MRI**.
- **Bruising or swelling over the lateral hip** is more typical of intertrochanteric fractures (blood can track outside the capsule). In intracapsular NOF fractures, swelling is minimal because the capsule contains the haematoma.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning
**Immediate (first hour):**
- **Analgesia:** Paracetamol 1g IV/PO + morphine sulphate 2.5-5mg IV (titrate to pain) or use a **femoral nerve block** (20mL 0.25% levobupivacaine) → excellent pain relief without respiratory depression. *Why?* Pain causes immobility, delirium, hypertension; nerve block reduces need for systemic opioids.
- **IV fluids:** 500-1000mL crystalloid unless heart failure. *Why?* Patient may be nil-by-mouth for surgery; preload prevents hypotension.
- **Remove traction** — traditional skin traction is **not recommended** (increases pain and pressure sores). *Pitfall:* Don’t apply traction; it does not improve outcome.
- **Assess for acute medical issues:** ECG, chest X-ray, bloods. *Why?* Identify decompensated HF, AF, pneumonia, anaemia that need optimization before theatre.
- **Notify anaesthetist and orthopaedic team** for expedited surgery. Target surgery within 48 hours (NICE guidance) — reduces mortality and complications.

**Ward (24-72h):**
- **Surgical decision:** Based on fracture type, patient mobility, and cognition.
  - **Undisplaced intracapsular (Garden I-II):** **Cannulated screws (Hansson/Pins)** — percutaneous fixation with 2-3 screws. Preserves the joint.
  - **Displaced intracapsular (Garden III-IV):**
    - **Patient >65 years, low mobility, pre-existing arthritis → Hemiarthroplasty** (replaces femoral head only). Choose cemented stem (less thigh pain, better fixation) or uncemented (if medical comorbidities prevent cement use — risk of cement reaction syndrome).
    - **Patient <65 years, high mobility, good bone quality → Total hip replacement (THR)** — better long-term outcome and lower risk of revision.
  - **Intertrochanteric (extracapsular):** **Sliding hip screw (SHS)** for stable patterns; **intramedullary nail (IM nail)** for unstable patterns (reverse obliquity, subtrochanteric extension).
- **Thromboprophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily for 28-35 days (if CrCl >30) or fondaparinux 2.5mg SC. *Why?* High risk of DVT/PE after hip fracture (incidence up to 60% without prophylaxis). Mechanical prophylaxis (stockings) can be used but LMWH is standard.
- **Mobilise day 1 post-op** with physiotherapy, weight-bearing as tolerated (for all fixation types except certain complex cases). *Why?* Prevents chest infection, muscle wasting, pressure sores. **Pitfall:** Delaying mobilisation increases complications.
- **Osteoporosis workup:** Start vitamin D 800IU daily + calcium 1g daily. Arrange DEXA scan 3 months post-op. Start bisphosphonate (alendronate 70mg PO weekly) once fracture healed (usually 2-4 weeks). *Why?* Second hip fracture risk is 20% within 5 years.
- **Delirium prevention:** Avoid benzodiazepines; ensure good pain control; regular orientation; early removal of catheters; keep glasses and hearing aids.

**Long-term:**
- **Falls risk assessment:** Identify home hazards, consider occupational therapy home visit.
- **Osteoporosis treatment continuation:** Bisphosphonate for 3-5 years; review yearly.
- **Follow-up X-ray** at 6 weeks and 3 months to assess union. If non-union or AVN develops, may need revision.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table
| Feature | NOF Fracture (Intracapsular) | Hip Dislocation (Posterior) | Trochanteric Bursitis | Osteoarthritis (Coxarthrosis) |
|---------|-----------------------------|-----------------------------|-----------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Acute, groin, on any hip motion; inability to weight-bear | Severe, at hip; leg held in internal rotation, adduction, shortened | Lateral hip pain, worse lying on side, tender over greater trochanter | Chronic, groin/buttock pain, worse with walking, stiffness |
| **Exam** | Shortened + externally rotated leg; painful passive ROM | Shortened + internally rotated leg; femoral head not palpable in posterior dislocation | Pain on resisted abduction; full ROM in non-inflammatory stage | Crepitus, reduced internal rotation first, fixed flexion deformity |
| **X-ray** | Fracture line through femoral neck; displacement seen | Femoral head out of acetabulum; usually posterior | Normal (may see trochanteric calcific tendinitis) | Joint space narrowing, osteophytes, subchondral cysts |
| **Labs** | Normal (unless pathological) | Normal | Normal | Normal |
| **Key differentiator** | Fracture line on X-ray; external rotation with shortening | Internal rotation + shortening; head not in joint on X-ray | Point tenderness over trochanter; pain with resisted abduction; no groin pain | Chronicity; X-ray changes; normal acute markers |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
**Avascular Necrosis (AVN) of the femoral head**
- **Presentation:** Delayed — months to years post-fracture. Worsening groin pain, limp, inability to weight-bear. May follow initial good recovery.
- **Key signs:** X-ray shows increased radiodensity (sclerosis) of the femoral head, later collapse/flattening (Ficat stages). MRI is definitive (low T1 signal, double-line sign on T2).
- **What to do:** Early AVN: core decompression or vascularised fibular graft in young patients. Late AVN: THR.

**Non-union**
- **Presentation:** Pain at fracture site >6 months post-op, inability to walk comfortably.
- **Key signs:** X-ray shows no bridging callus, gap at fracture site, implant loosening.
- **What to do:** Usually requires revision fixation, often with bone grafting or conversion to arthroplasty.

**Deep wound infection / periprosthetic joint infection**
- **Presentation:** Persistent pain, wound discharge, fever, raised CRP (can be >100).
- **Key signs:** Erythema, purulent drainage, sinus tract. Aspiration shows organism (Staph aureus most common).
- **What to do:** I&D with retention of implant (if early, <2 weeks) or 2-stage revision (remove implant, antibiotic spacer, then re-implant after 6 weeks of IV antibiotics).

**DVTs / PE**
- **Presentation:** Unilateral leg swelling, warmth, tenderness (DVT); sudden dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, hypoxia, collapse (PE).
- **Key signs:** Wells score >2; D-dimer positive but not specific; CTPA confirms PE.
- **What to do:** Therapeutic LMWH (enoxaparin 1.5mg/kg SC daily) or DOAC (rivaroxaban 15mg BD). Consider IVC filter if anticoagulation contraindicated.

**Pressure sores**
- **Presentation:** Discoloration, ulceration over sacrum, heels, elbows.
- **What to do:** Regular turning (4-hourly), pressure-relieving mattress, early mobilisation. *Pitfall:* Delay to theatre increases risk — skin can break within 24h.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
**Mnemonics:**
- **GARDEN** for intracapsular fracture classification:
  - **G**rade 1 – impacted
  - **A**ngulated (Grade 3 – partially displaced)
  - **R**eally displaced (Grade 4 – fully displaced)
  - **D**on’t forget Grade 2 (complete but undisplaced)
  - **E**valuation of blood supply risk (Grades 3&4 high)
  - **N**ecessary management decision (replacement vs fixation)
- **LEGS** for signs of displaced hip fracture:
  - **L**eg shortened
  - **E**xternal rotation
  - **G**roin pain
  - **S**upport (cannot weight-bear)
- **3Ms** for complications to watch: Mortality (30%), Morbidity (delirium, PE), Mobility loss.

**Clinical Pearls:**
1. An elderly patient who cannot straight-leg-raise after a fall has a hip fracture until proven otherwise — even with normal X-ray. Order an MRI.
2. The most reliable sign of an impacted (Garden I) fracture is **pain on axial loading through the foot** (heel strike) with no leg deformity.
3. Always check the **contralateral hip** — patients with one hip fracture have a 20% risk of a second within 5 years.
4. Never give an NSAID for pain in a patient with renal impairment (common in elderly) — risk of AKI.
5. In a patient on warfarin, reverse with vitamin K (2.5-5mg IV) and/or prothrombin complex concentrate if urgent surgery needed — but weigh risk of thrombosis.
6. The most commonly missed fracture on X-ray is the **non-displaced intracapsular** — the only clue may be a subtle disruption of Shenton’s line (the curve formed by the obturator foramen and femoral neck).
7. **Delirium** is the most common complication (>50% of patients) — often multi-factorial (pain, sleep deprivation, dehydration, infection). Don't blame “dementia” — address causes.

**Common Ward / Exam Mistakes:**
- Ordering a **CT scan instead of MRI** for an occult hip fracture — CT has lower sensitivity for marrow oedema; you may miss it.
- Applying **skin traction** — it increases pain and pressure sores without benefit.
- Assuming a **normal X-ray after trauma** means no fracture — if patient cannot weight-bear, get MRI.
- Delaying **osteoporosis treatment** — start vitamin D and calcium in hospital; arrange DEXA before discharge.
- Not checking **pre-operative group & save** until the night before — delays surgery.

### 10. Escalation Criteria
**When to call your senior (Registrar or Consultant):**
- **NEWS2 score ≥5** — especially if high respiratory rate, low SpO2, or hypotension.
- **Acute confusion / agitation** not settling with simple measures — could be hypoxia, PE, hypoglycaemia, or infection.
- **Chest pain or sudden dyspnoea** — suspect PE (especially day 3-7 post-op).
- **Hypoxia (SpO2 <92% on air)** — check for PE, pneumonia, atelectasis.
- **Temperature ≥38.5°C** — likely infection (chest, urine, wound); note that fracture alone can cause mild fever (day 1-2) but should not persist.
- **Uncontrolled pain** despite nerve block and systemic analgesia — may indicate compartment syndrome (rare in hip), infection, or patient anxiety.
- **Wound oozing or haematoma** with signs of bleeding (tachycardia, hypotension) — may need return to theatre.

**When to call the ITU/MET/CCOT:**
- **NEWS2 ≥7** in any patient.
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** unexplained.
- **GCS ≤12** or acute drop of ≥2 points.
- **Respiratory rate >30 or <8**.
- **Systolic BP <90 mmHg despite fluid bolus** — suspect septic shock, PE, or bleeding.
- **Cardiac arrest** — immediately call 2222.

**Specific thresholds to remember:**
- **Hb <70 g/L** (or <80 in active ischaemia) → consider transfusion.
- **Creatinine increase >26 µmol/L in 24h** or >50% from baseline → suspect AKI; discuss with renal team.
- **INR >1.5** pre-op → reverse with vitamin K (and consider PCC if need urgent surgery).
- **Potassium <2.5 or >6.5 mmol/L** → urgent correction and ECG; discuss with med reg.

Remember: The senior clinician can often be reached by simply stating the NEWS2, the problem, and your concern — “Mr/Smith has NEWS 6, with acute hypoxia and confusion day 3 post-hip fracture; I’m worried about a PE.”
